Psychoanalysis on the Move: The Work of Joseph Sandler

Edited by Peter Fonagy, Arnold Cooper, and Robert Wallerstein. London and New York: Routledge, 1999, 235 pp., $90.00 clothbound, $29.99 paperback.

Batya Friesel

Batya Friesel

3 Yiftach Street
Jerusalem 93503
Israel
Fax #: +972-2-671-7917

Batya Friesel   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, Israel Institute of Psychoanalysis; Lecturer, psychotherapy program, Hebrew University, Jerusalem.

Psychoanalysis on the Move is a collection of fifteen articles by leading psychoanalytic thinkers of different traditions from around the world. It was intended to serve as a comprehensive and accessible overview of Joseph Sandler's contribution of to the development of

690

psychoanalysis. In over forty years of intensive psychoanalytic practice and theorizing, Sandler produced a tremendous volume of writings. His influence was felt also in a long career of teaching in clinical and academic forums, as well as in a variety of administrative roles, both in England and abroad, in which he actively worked for the advancement of psychoanalysis. In the light of Sandler's unexpected death in October 1998, the book now also stands as a tribute to his memory.

Sandler's psychoanalytic work has had a decisive impact both on clinical theory and metapsychology. Many of his clinical concepts, particularly “role-responsiveness” and “the background of safety,” have been integrated into the mainstream of psychoanalytic theory, and his metapsychological works have served as a basis for the study of the foundations and structure of Freud's thought, as well as those of later, neo-Freudian thinking. His attempts to clarify, sharpen, and integrate the basic concepts and formulations of psychoanalysis are a striking feature of his work. Psychoanalysis on the Move brings to the fore not only the continuing relevance of Sandler's innovations and conceptualizations, but in a much more subtle way points also, as the title perhaps implies, to the evolution of psychoanalytic thinking and the part Sandler has played in it. This book ultimately demonstrates that one of the principal ways in which psychoanalysis evolves is through an ongoing process of reflection in individuals such as Sandler. This reflection is an attempt to understand the clinical encounter as experienced in the course of one's life and, through careful study, to integrate one's understanding with that of others. This is a gradual process, rather than a radical one.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Uses of the Concept of Primary Femininity

Diane Martinez

Diane Martinez

102 Cave Lane
San Antonio, TX 78209
Fax: 210-829-7892
E-mail: Dlmpsych@aol.com

Diane Martinez  

Nancy Kulish opened the panel by noting that concepts of primary femininity have influenced a broad range of contemporary psychoanalytic writings on female development and psychology. She roughly defined the term primary femininity as referring to the idea that a little girl forms a sense of herself and her body first as a female. This, of course, contrasts with early psychoanalytic theory, in which female psychosexual development was conceptualized as initially masculine, with further development dependent on renunciation of that primary masculinity. As such, femininity was regarded as a difficult achievement, one based in a sense of deficiency. Many, including contemporaries of Freud such as Homey, Jones, and Greenacre, have criticized this theory as limiting our ability to understand female patients and have expressed contrary views.

However, as Kulish explained, the term primary femininity was first used in a different frame of reference. Stoller coined the term initially to refer to gender identity arising out of an early maternal matrix for both males and females. Chodorow's early writings, in keeping with this idea, considered the differential effects, for girls and for boys, of separation from same-sex and opposite-sex objects.

Kulish outlined how use of the concept of primary femininity to refer to primary gender identify focused psychoanalytic attention on a positive sense of femaleness and the role of the mother. Tyson has explored this area extensively. Bernstein, Mayer, and Richards have considered the bodily aspects of a primary sense of femininity that may underlie female genital anxieties. Further, writers such as Jones, Deutsch, and Parens have considered the concept in relation to bisexuality and homosexuality.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 17, 1999.

Panelists: Nancy Kulish (chair), Rosemary Balsam, Elizabeth Lloyd Mayer, Judith A. Yanof. Discussants: Ruth Fischer, Rhonda Shaw.

1379

Horst Kächele

Horst Kächele   Professor, Department of Psychotherapy and Psychosomatic Medicine, University of Ulm; Center for Psychotherapy Research, Stuttgart.

Psychoanalytic outcome research got off to a good start when the Berlin Institute commissioned Fenichel and his colleagues to prepare an evaluation of therapeutic outcomes. In 1930 they jointly and proudly published their report. Things looked less cheerful when Eysenck (1952) published a very negative evaluation of the Berlin data. Bergin (1971), however, summarized his own evaluation of the Berlin data as follows: “The four divergent but equally reasonable tabulations of the Berlin data clearly establish my point that there is no valid way to assess the effect of psychoanalysis from the information available. I can see no clear justification for choosing one interpretation over another, even though I do have personal biases in certain directions. The ambiguity in these data cannot be resolved” (p. 225).

Bergin's careful and sophisticated evaluation contained some other observations, namely that the divergence of the tabulations was more pronounced with regard to the psychoanalytic treatments than to the more eclectic therapies. Even though Bergin does not identify himself as a friend of psychoanalysis, he concludes that the results up to 1952, when Eysenck published his paper, must be considered encouraging, if not dramatic: “It is of particular interest, however, that the longer and more intensive the treatment, the better the results” (p. 227).

Now we are in the year 2001, thirty years later. Why are we still in doubt about the outcome of psychoanalytic therapies? In their introduction, the authors of this timely book state that “over the past thirty years psychoanalysis has lost much status. No longer the sole rational therapy for psychological distress, today it competes with many other treatments” (p. x).

1041

Psychoanalytic Understanding of Violence and Suicide

Edited by Rosine Jozef Perelberg. London: Routledge, 1999, vii + 177 pp., $95.00 hardcover, $29.99 paperback.

Stuart Twemlow

Stuart Twemlow

25 Main Street
P.O. Box 962
Stockbridge, MA 01262-0962
Fax: 413-298-4020
E-mail: Stuart.Twemlow@Austenriggs.net

Stuart Twemlow   Director, Erik Erikson Institute for Education and Research, Austen Riggs Center, Stockbridge, MA; faculty, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Codirector, Peaceful Schools Project, Menninger Clinic, Topeka.

This volume, the thirty-third in the New Library of Psychoanalysis, a project of the Institute of Psychoanalysis in London, includes eight contributors, seven of whom are members of the British Psychoanalytical

334

Society. The foreword is by Leonard Shengold, whose contribution stands in stark contrast to the others by virtue of its ego psychological, more North American viewpoint. Shengold makes no substantive comments on the other papers in the volume. Whatever his silence means politically, this courageous book represents a distillation of the best British thinking on a topic derived from work by the Young Adults Research Group of the Anna Freud Centre. Six patients are presented, two of whom were suicidal and four violent to others. The clinical vignettes vividly capture the inner life of extremely violent individuals, several of whom it is highly unlikely would ever have been considered suitable for intensive psychoanalysis in North America. These authors are to be congratulated for their heroic efforts with these dangerous patients.

The theme of all the chapters is object-relational. Thus, the relationship with the analyst, in the here-and-now understanding and experiencing of transference and countertransference interchanges, is what is regarded as healing for the patient. The concept of violence is defined to include not only the intentional infliction of bodily harm on others, but also the entertaining of certain violent fantasies that complicate and distort the meaning of “others” to the violent individual. A central issue in the book is the notion of the core complex, an intense longing for union with the caregiving object, leaving the individual with a paradoxical fear of being merged and annihilated and yet with an intense longing for that union. Thus, for all of the authors, violence is in essence an attempt to cope with the experience of feeling overwhelmed by the caregiving object.

The book begins with a literature review by Perelberg, summarizing the various transatlantic positions in the debate on aggression, concluding with several useful ways of distinguishing violence and aggression, setting the scene for the later chapters. Next, Fonagy and Target's groundbreaking paper on the role of the body and the father in violence is reprinted, establishing an important linking influence: the concept of mentalization, or the capacity to think about mental states in self and others. According to Fonagy, violent individuals and borderline patients lack mentalizing skills as the result of very early developmental failure to see themselves reflected in their primary caregiving objects, leading to failure in self-development and manifested in adult life by a failure to empathize with others as separate from themselves.

Freud and the Institution of Psychoanalytic Knowledge

Sarah Winter: Stanford: Stanford University Press, 1999, xvi + 385 pp., $55.00 hardbound, $19.95 paperback.

Jeffrey Berman

Jeffrey Berman

1400 Washington Avenue
Albany, NY 1222
E-mail: Jberman@cnsvax.albany.edu

Jeffrey Berman   Professor of English, State University of New York at Albany.

Sarah Winter begins her cultural history of psychoanalysis with a question posed in the early 1990s by a Time magazine cover article: “Is Freud dead?” Her answer is that Freud's spirit—expressed in the discipline that is his offspring—remains most assuredly alive, though not in the form its creator had intended. Winter is concerned neither with deifying nor debunking Freud; others have done one or both. Nor is she interested in evaluating psychoanalysis as a scientific theory or its effectiveness as a therapy, questions that lie outside her expertise as a literary and cultural scholar. Rather, she examines the strategies Freud used to ensure the institutional survival of psychoanalysis—an examination that leads her on a daunting intellectual journey into the fields of literary criticism, cultural studies, historical sociology, and epistemology.

Winter's methodology is influenced by the French sociologist Pierre Bourdieu, who argues that a person's embodied history incorporates expectations, desires, and goals shaped by social and familial forces. She is indebted to other prominent French intellectuals, including Michel Foucault, whose analysis of power has been so influential in the second half of the twentieth century. Winter maintains that it is necessary to step outside of a discipline, in this case psychoanalysis, in order to examine its history and development. She is part of a growing cultural movement that seeks to reassess Freud's role in shaping the direction of psychoanalysis.

Winter focuses on three related strategies Freud adopted in his writings to institutionalize psychoanalytic knowledge and practice. The first involved incorporating into psychoanalytic theory elements of classical Greek tragedy so that canonical literature would lend prestige and universality to his emerging theory.

709

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unfree Associations: Inside Psychoanalytic Institutes.

Douglas Kirsner. London: Process Press, 2000, 324 pp., $32.00.

This volume presents detailed histories of organizational conflicts in psychoanalytic institutes, based on in-depth interviews with participants of varied analytic persuasions. Difficulties that Kirsner sees as going back to Freud are closely detailed as they have unfolded across the years in the troubles of psychoanalytic institutes and societies in New York, Boston, Chicago, and Los Angeles. The author's long and intense study succeeds in bringing together and laying open for consideration those complex personal and theoretical struggles that have plagued psychoanalytic organizations to a degree that at times seems like a fratricidal war.

While the author has clearly labored to maintain an evenhanded approach to the battles he records, the fact that most of his data derive from personal interviews means that some coloring of reports out of personal animus inevitably spills into the text from time to time. Nonetheless, the study provides the principal and thus the best source available to those who were not present during the conflicts considered.

In his final chapter the author draws personal conclusions and makes some recommendations. He sees the difficulties of organized psychoanalysis as arising from the twin facts (in his view) that analysis is not a science, and that it is not a religion. His study of the problems intrinsic to power structures serves as a cautionary tale that commands respectful consideration, while his personal preference for a fully free and open marketplace of ideas—despite its appeal—gives scant respect to the serious problems inherent in efforts to maintain standards of education.

1087

[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Psychotherapy and Learning in Clinical Supervision

Edited by Rosemary H. Balsam. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2001, 318 pp., $40.00.

Despite a history of public engagement (evidenced in contributions to innovative care of the military during World War II and in leadership of psychiatric education in the middle of the last century), American psychoanalysts have long been seen as insular—limited in their activities to the confines of their analytic offices and their isolated institutes.

1089

What truth there was in that image has been drastically changed in recent years by the opening of new psychoanalytic institutes, the growth of extension divisions, and the movement of analytic practitioners to wider participation in the community at large.

This volume is one of the fruits of this new movement, and originates in a discussion group for clinical supervisors that was conducted in the early nineties by Rosemary Balsam under the aegis of the Western New England Society for Psychoanalysis. Contributions from twenty experienced supervisors are here brought together. The first half of the book addresses aspects of supervision beyond formal psychoanalyses: such as problems resulting from the current medicalization of psychotherapy, issues related to managed care, use of drugs as part of the therapeutic intervention, emergency situations, work in day care settings, etc. The second half deals more directly with the nature of the work of supervision.

Not directly concerned with psychoanalysis proper, this text grows from and adds to the richness that results when psychoanalytic principles are carried into wider areas of clinical application.

1090

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Letter from Italy

Gemma Fiumara

Gemma Fiumara

Piazza Stefano Jacini 5
Rome 00191
Italy
E-mail: Corradi@phil.uniroma3.it

Gemma Fiumara  

I shall make an attempt to report on the ongoing conversations of Italian analysts, rather than trying to provide a presentation of our Society. I could not think of describing identifiable political and scientific developments, or of supplying statistics, names, and data. Immersed as I am in the psychoanalytic community, it is not easy to attain a sufficiently clear perspectival view. I shall thus try to share my understanding of the concerns, achievements, and hopes that emerge from the life of the psychoanalytic community, which is a very lively community indeed.

What would strike any visitor, in fact, and what challenges any Italian analyst, is the very great number of scientific events that are constantly being organized. An unprecedented number of national and international meetings is going on in the country, and often two or more interesting conferences are scheduled on the same date. The choice of what to attend and where to go seems a constant challenge for each one of us. At psychoanalytic institutes in Bologna, Florence, Genoa, Naples, Milan, Palermo, Rome, Turin, and Venice there are meetings nearly every evening: discussions of clinical cases; visiting analysts, some foreign; presentations of books; study groups focusing on such specific themes as, for instance, the psychoses, perversions, gender identity, food problems, neurosciences, and dreams.

Some colleagues think that such a very high frequency of meetings, conferences, and colloquia can be interpreted as a search for psychic comfort or a reinforcement of professional identity for analysts emerging from long days of involvement in a very peculiar sort of work. The intensity of shared scientific life, moreover, seems to dissipate the tendency to feel like permanent disciples of certain analysts who appear, or are made to appear, to possess some superior knowledge or skill. Especially among the younger colleagues, there is an inclination to be extremely sincere and specific about clinical experiences. This attitude is increasingly interwoven with a critical view of the ever successful starlike figures of psychoanalytic culture who are now often looked at with benign curiosity, rather than with old-fashioned admiration.

responseSet.title: September 11, 2001
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

September 11, 2001

This issue, the last of our forty-ninth year of publication, was still in production at the time of the events of September 11. Reflecting on those events, Martin Bergmann writes, “It is conceivable, although it may be too early to know, that September 11, 2001, will go down in history as one of those dates that change the flow of history, that it will rank with July 14, 1789 (the storming of the Bastille), or with August 2, 1914, when Germany declared war on Russia to ignite World War I, a date historians regard as the true beginning of the twentieth century.”

Although the theme of this issue is “examining gender and sexuality”—subjects on their face far removed from terrorism (though see Donald Moss's article on racism, homophobia, and misogyny, as well as Ethel Spector Person's reflections on group psychology)—we are mindful that the shadow of September 11 has fallen on us all, readers and contributors alike. Future issues of JAPA will undoubtedly address September 11 in ways we might expect—papers on the psychological consequences of terror, loss, and bereavement, or on the psychology of international and cultural conflicts—as well as in ways we cannot predict. However, since the lag time between submission and publication is often more than a year, many of us may feel the need for a more immediate forum in which to express our thoughts and feelings.

We have therefore decided to publish the paper by Martin Bergmann quoted above—“Psychoanalytic Reflections on September 11, 2001,” a work in progress—on the JAPA Psa-NETCAST. We hope that it will stimulate wide-ranging discussion of issues relevant for psychoanalysis, both as a profession and as an intellectual and scientific endeavor. The editorial board of JAPA and other distinguished colleagues will serve as faculty, with Robert White moderating the discussion. Martin Bergmann's responses will be posted on the NETCAST as part of the ongoing exchange. We hope that readers and colleagues here and abroad will take this opportunity to articulate their reactions to these profound changes in our world. (To log on, see next page.)

[[RunningHead]]

Freudian Analysts/Feminist Issues

Judith M. Hughes. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1999, x + 222 pp., $40.00.

Arlene Richards

Arlene Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128-4308
Fax: 212-427-0585
E-mail: 73441.3427@compuserve.com

Arlene Richards   Fellow of IPTAR; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society.

This highly personal selection of analysts taking up feminist issues affords the reader a useful look at some of the issues that have concerned analysts trying to create a theory of female psychology that will allow us to deal appropriately with women patients in analysis. Hughes begins with a theoretician of female sexuality frequently cited by feminists as an apologist for Freud—Helene Deutsch. But rather than repudiating Deutsch, Hughes shows her work as a progression of ideas that attempt to take into account both Deutsch's own experience as a woman and her experiences as a physician, a psychiatrist, and a psychoanalyst. Hughes asserts that Deutsch's work was influenced by her involvement with problems experienced by women during her time in Boston. The social situation that made women's lives difficult formed the view of women's choices and therefore of their conflicts. While the dualities of passive-active, feminine-masculine, and heterosexual-homosexual reflected her heritage from Freud, Deutsch pointed to the dualities of nature-culture and species-individual as salient. Hughes reminds us that Deutsch's own contribution was to articulate and privilege the dualities of masochism-narcissism and motherliness-eroticism. In this regard, Deutsch speaks to the modern American woman. Have a baby or a career, how much time for home, how much for the office, does it hurt the child if the mother works at home? Does a woman lose credibility at work when she refuses to travel? Deutsch talked of balance between the masochism necessary for the conception, bearing, and rearing of children and the narcissism necessary to close the study door when the child wants more of mother and the song calls to be written. Or is it better to leave home for work so that the boundary is clear to the child? Such questions reduce to the duality of masochism-narcissism.

Deutsch was equally ruthless and clear-sighted in posing the conflict between motherliness and eroticism.

[[RunningHead]]

The Unknown Karen Horney: Essays on Gender, Culture, and Psychoanalysis.

Edited by Bernard J. Paris. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2000, 384 pp., $35.00.

Joyce Lerner

Joyce Lerner

301 East 79th Street, #19A
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: Joycella@aol.com

Joyce Lerner   Training and Supervising Analyst, American Institute for Psychoanalysis; Director, Metropolitan Institute for Training in Psychoanalytic Therapy; Associate Editor, American Journal of Psychoanalysis.

With the publication of this volume, along with some previously published but inaccessible writings, all of Karen Horney's hitherto unpublished works are now in print. Bernard J. Paris, a literary critic and Homey biographer and scholar who has long devoted himself to Horneyan studies, has collected and painstakingly edited the compilation. Where necessary, he has overseen translations from the German. Paris introduces each of the two parts of the book as well as each paper. His comments place the contributions in the context of the evolution of psychoanalysis and of Horney's development. With his illuminating commentary, Paris expands our appreciation of Horney and her work.

Part 1 contains Horney's papers on feminine psychology and the relations between the sexes through three periods: from her perspective as a female Freudian analyst, from the vantage of her shift from Freud's model toward object relations and the impact of culture, and from the standpoint of her mature theory. Her writings address such topics as love, marriage, sadism, sexuality, menstrual disorders, and pregnancy. Paris clarifies her eventual move away from emphasizing female psychology and culture.

Part II is devoted to Horney's other writings as she created and refined her own theory. It includes full papers as well as abstracts, symposia, and brief items. Especially notable in this section are Horney's “classic” papers, which for the first time are available to a wider psychoanalytic audience. “The Value of Vindictiveness” is an exposition of the defensive importance of vengefulness. “The Paucity of Inner Experiences” describes the sense of inner emptiness that results from distancing from all that one thinks, feels, and is. “On Feeling Abused” explores the personal sense of being victimized. Paris notes that the last paper is particularly relevant in the current climate, in which perceptions of victimization are often unquestioningly accepted as valid. Also collected here is Horney's letter of resignation from the New York Psychoanalytic Society. In it she describes what continues within institutes to be a battle, the clash between “classicists” and “nonclassicists” over what shall be taught, how much say candidates have over their own education, and to what extent dissidents can be tolerated.

[[RunningHead]]

The Single Woman-Married Man Syndrome

Richard Tuch. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2000, xxiii + 310 pp., $30.00.

Jeffrey Golland

Jeffrey Golland

145 Fourth Avenue
New York, NY 10003

Jeffrey Golland   Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of the New York Freudian Society; Visiting Professor of Education, Lehman College, City University of New York.

Identification of a syndrome in either the medical or the behavioral realm is a matter of great import, with both practical and theoretical consequences. An interpersonal syndrome identified by a traditionally trained psychoanalyst would be a major contribution. This book's title not only promises a syndrome, but the particular area of study has social significance and would likely draw a wide audience. If the work succeeds, both the public and the profession of psychoanalysis would gain. There is a risk, however. If science and scholarship do not support the assertion of discovery, the work may amount to little more than sensationalism.

This book by a Los Angeles training and supervising analyst has as its general topic extramarital affairs, and its ambition in part is to provide a window into related male-female issues and the nature of love. Richard Tuch's thesis is that the single woman–married man syndrome is specific and includes particular patterns of behavior, feelings, and attitudes not previously described in the literature. The syndrome has an incidence, a typology, and particular dynamic constellations. It is unlike most psychoanalytic syndromes in being essentially interpersonal; only folie à deux and sadomasochism are similar, in that they involve two persons with complementary intersubjective states. The key dynamic issue for both parties is interpersonal power and control.

Tuch provides a clear descriptive overview, and full-length chapters presenting his data from each of three different perspectives: the single woman's, the married man's, and the wife's. He then focuses on the issues of marital discord and extramarital affairs as significant social-psychological phenomena within which this syndrome resides. Tuch's concluding chapters are on gender, treatment implications, and the nature of man-woman love.

The author makes clear that any observations on the topics he addresses are culture-bound, and that monogamy is not the human norm. He is also assiduous in his several disclaimers regarding his evidence and the generalizations he draws. He takes an analytic attitude toward his subject matter, one that avoids blame, dismissive stereotyping, and voyeurism. He seeks to specify the unconscious nature of the motivation of the parties to the syndrome.

responseSet.title: A Letter from Argentina
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Letter from Argentina

Raquel de Salvarezza

Raquel de Salvarezza

Fax: +54-11-4815-3778
E-mail: Lsalvare@psi.uba.ar

Raquel de Salvarezza  

First, I hope that you and those close to you are all right. I am horrified by the destruction that befell New York and Washington. I share in your grief as I recall the attack on the Israeli embassy here in Buenos Aires and on the AMIA. We relive those other tragic moments each time we hear of a new terrorist act.

Perhaps this is indeed an appropriate time to furnish you the history you have requested. Perhaps turning our thoughts to the psycho-analytic pioneers in Argentina may prove not only a welcome distraction but an inspiration. From those pioneers we received a memorable heritage: the enthusiasm and courage with which they founded the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association. Let's not forget that during those years Argentina was still “neutral” in regard to Nazism, and that it was difficult to act or even think. Freedom was restricted, and authoritarianism and anti-Semitism were rampant. Yet these pioneers overcame their very real fears in their desire to learn the new science of the soul.

Freud's works were translated into Spanish in 1923. When Nazi persecution worsened in Vienna in 1933, a group of writers invited Freud to come to Argentina. Freud declined, saying that although he was able to read and understand Spanish he didn't feel capable of writing in that language. At the same time, several young physicians and other Argentine intellectuals began to passionately study psychoanalysis. You know their now famous names: Arnaldo and Matilde Rascovsky, Enrique Pichon Riviere, Aminda Aberastury (wife of Pichon Riviere), Alberto Tallaferro, Ferrari Hardoy, Flora Scolni, and a few others.

In 1938, fleeing Nazism and the war, Angel Garma and Celes Carcamo arrived in Buenos Aires. Both had been trained in Europe; very soon their practice was filled by those avid Argentine intellectuals who anxiously awaited the chance to develop clinical skills. The fact that founders, teachers, and students shared a native tongue must have contributed to the enormous expansion of Argentine psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: JAPA on the Cutting Edge
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

JAPA on the Cutting Edge

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards  

Psychoanalysis in the United States and abroad has been marked by periods of consolidation and periods of expansion. Freud has been faulted by some for his tight hold on its organizational and institutional development during his lifetime, but his efforts may have been necessary to assure the survival of his revolutionary discipline. Similarly, the sponsoring organization of this journal, the American Psychoanalytic Association, has been faulted in the past for exclusionary practices and overly rigid organizational and credentialing structures. But as Marvin Margolis states in the presidential address that opens this issue, the association has moved during the past two decades from consolidation to expansion, liberalization, and greater openness in matters both intellectual and organizational.

Some think that psychoanalysis is under siege, both as a science and as a therapeutic practice. Others feel, as I do, that our discipline rests on a solid basis of knowledge about the human psyche, and that this knowledge should be disseminated among the broader scientific community and the general public.

This issue, the first of our forty-ninth year of continuous publication, includes two papers that even before their publication here have received attention from the popular media. Edward Jeffery's study on the longevity of psychoanalysts was the subject of a piece on WebMD (an Internet health site) and in the New York Post. Brian Johnson, who contributes a study on addiction, dreaming, and drive, has been interviewed at length by a national news magazine. We are grateful to Dottie Jeffries, the association's media consultant, and Leon Hoffman, chair of its Public Information Committee, for their untiring efforts to assure that studies such as these receive the wide notice they deserve.

The research papers in this issue, which are introduced by Wilma Bucci, demonstrate once again that JAPA is on the cutting edge of psychoanalytic science. On page 340

[[RunningHead]]

The Treatment

Daniel Menaker. New York: Washington Square Press, 1998, 288 pp., $23.00 hardcover, $14.00 paperback.

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore Jacobs

170 East 77th Street (#1G)
New York, NY 10021

Theodore Jacobs   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute.

Character may indeed be fate, but caricature would seem the fate of most fictional psychoanalysts. From F. Scott Fitzgerald's troubled hero, Dr. Dick Diver, to Sailboat Selzer, Lillian Ross's send-up of sixties analysts, to the latterday creations of Philip Roth, Judith Rossner, and Adam Gropnick, most often the analyst of story and novel tends to be a character tethered to a stereotype, the fictional incarnation of whatever popular notion of analysts is current at the time.

While unquestionably a caricature, Ernesto Morales, the antihero of Daniel Menaker's amusing, intriguing, and provocative novel The Treatment, is no stereotype. Quite the opposite. He is, in fact, like no other analyst one is likely to encounter, in life or in literature.

One of the more outrageous creations in modern fiction, rather like an imaginary mix of Bruno Bettelheim and Señor Wences, Morales is a refugee Cuban, a Catholic Freudian analyst whose office is adorned with a crucifix and whose preferred way of working is to nail his patients, hands and feet, to the flimsy planks of their own rationalizations.

The patient in this case is Jake Singer, a thirty-something high school English teacher who is floundering badly in life. Isolated, joyless, unable to love, Jake is still grieving the mother he lost in childhood. The novel chronicles Jake's growth from this condition of misery and unproductive self-involvement to a place in life in which he is not only happily married and is a successful and mature educator, but has become a generative person able to give to others.

To accomplish this, Jake has had to come to terms with the devastating loss that he suffered in childhood, and by helping others who have suffered similar losses—all of the major characters in the novel have had to contend with such traumatic experiences—he has found a way to repair himself.

What seems initially, then, a straightforward and familiar tale of a young man growing into responsible manhood, turns out to be much more than that. Fundamentally, Jake's story is about pain and loss and their sequelae in life. It raises questions about the ways in which individuals who have suffered profound losses can recover from them, about the price these losses exact, about their enduring impact, and about how one lives with the reality of such painful loss.

Richard Friedman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Medical College, Cornell University Medical School; Lecturer in Psychiatry, Columbia Presbyterian Medical Center, New York State Psychiatric Institute.

This important book, written not by a psychoanalyst but by a journalist, is painful to read. It tells two interweaving stories. One depicts a struggle to cope with great suffering by a child and his family following a devastating surgical error that occurred when he was eight months old. The other narrative line concerns a scientific debate about the effects of nature and nurture on gender identity development. This is given dramatic form because it involved a struggle between two brilliant men, John Money and Milton Diamond.

In 1965 a young Canadian couple became the parents of identical twin boys. During infancy the children developed symptoms of phimosis, a condition in which the foreskin contracts around the tip of the urethra, and whose treatment is circumcision. Rather than performing the ordinarily innocuous procedure with a scalpel, the physician used a cautery device. Tragically, the entire penis of one child was burned and “over the next few days, baby Bruce's penis dried and broke away in pieces” (p. 15). Understandably, it was decided not to circumcise the other twin, Brian, whose urethral symptoms remitted without treatment. Limitations of surgical technique were such that construction of a new, functional penis was not then possible. Medical authorities advised the parents that their son would never be able to live a normal life, and would have to resign himself to the role of a physically defective outcast.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Homosexuality and Psychoanalysis

Edited by Tim Dean and Christopher Lane. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2001, x + 466 pp., $25.00 paperback.

Eleanor Schuker

Eleanor Schuker

150 West End Avenue, #26A
New York, NY 10023
Fax: 212-595-9111
E-mail: Ess3@columbia.edu

Eleanor Schuker   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University.

Because psychoanalytic knowledge about the homosexualities has long been clouded by political forces and prejudice, open-minded psychoanalytic clinicians should be eager for new insights on this topic. Decades of psychoanalytic theorizing misappropriated moralistic anti-sexual attitudes, allowing prejudice and coercion. More recently, oppositional forces of political correctness and an exclusive focus on the pathogenicity of homophobic attitudes have contributed to a similar constriction of scientific investigation. Many basic questions in this area remain unresolved. How do such factors as biological forces, identifications, familial cognitive direction and seduction, a child's use of sexuality to resolve developmental conflicts, cultural pressures toward conformity, and adaptational needs to develop a sociosexual identity contribute to an individual psyche? Does structure building involving gender identity, gender role consolidation, and oedipal resolution serve an essential role in maturation toward capacities for relating and loving? Are there a variety of possible pathways for consolidation of object choice during development, for both males and females, and is such consolidation crucial for psychic health? Is bisexuality a developmental potential, or is it actively ongoing in adult psychic life? Does development require conformity and therefore entail some loss?

1462

Betrayed as Boys: Psychodynamic Treatment of Sexually Abused Men

Richard B. Gartner. New York: Guilford Press, 1999, 324 pp., $42.00 hardcover, $22.00 paperback.

Bonnie Buchele

Bonnie Buchele

4320 Wornall Road, Suite 452
Kansas City, MO 64111
Fax: 816-531-1117
E-mail: Drbuchele@kc.rr.com

Bonnie Buchele   faculty, Greater Kansas City Psychoanalytic Institute; President, American Group Psychotherapy Association.

Publications about men sexually abused as boys are infrequent, but rare indeed is the work that discusses this population, clinically and theoretically, in sound psychoanalytic terms. Here, in Richard Gartner's Betrayed as Boys, we have such a volume. Gartner writes with a clear psychoanalytic understanding of the conflicts and struggles of these men, and by presenting an array of vivid clinical examples, brings the reader to the experience of sitting in the consulting room, working with them to understand their pain. In great detail he explores the effects of victimization on masculinity and the role that societal myths—e.g., that “real” males are never victims—play in their gender development. Careful distinction is made between these myths and a more nuanced clinical reality: e.g., the myth that sexual abuse “turns” a boy gay is countered by the idea that it affects the quality of his sexual relationships, regardless of orientation; the myth that all sexually abused boys become abusers themselves gives way to the observation that a defensively heightened rigidity in an abused boy's masculine gender role (i.e., emotional constriction and deficits in empathy) increases the likelihood the boy will become abusive later in life. The chapter on dissociation and multiple self-states, which deals also with difficulties in affect regulation, is especially well presented.

Emphasis is placed on distinguishing the effects of sexual abuse on men from its effects on women, and Gartner's explication of its consequences for males is excellent. The book would have perhaps been improved, though, had the author said more about the similarities across the genders, in both subsequent psychopathology and complications in treatment; at times his overemphasis on gender differences strikes the reader as strained and unnecessarily dichotomizing.

1451

[[RunningHead]]

A Letter from Switzerland

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

Rudolfstr. 6
8008 Zürich,
Switzerland
246 Eliot Street
Brookline, MA 02467
E-mail: Csh105695@aol.com

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau  

Switzerland is a small country with four official languages, three of which our colleagues from the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society (SPS) work in—German, French, and Italian. The fourth is Raeto-Romansch. There are six training institutes: Zürich, Basel, and Bern in the German-speaking part; Geneva and Lausanne in the French-speaking region; and Lugano in the Italian-speaking canton. The SPS itself has 155 members, 91 and 64 of whom work in the French- and German-speaking parts of the country, respectively. There are 43 training and supervising analysts (66% of whom work in French-speaking institutes), and 322 candidates (including many older candidates who have chosen not to apply for membership). Scientific meetings of the whole society are held in the middle of the country in the capital city, Bern. They are conducted in French and German, and simultaneous translations are provided for paper presentations and discussions.

These facts are meaningful, and a crucial determinant of the specifics of the SPS. From early on it developed in two main branches. One grew in Zürich's famous Psychiatric University Hospital “Burghölzli,” where Eugen Bleuler opened the doors to psychoanalysis, and where C.G. Jung presented a lecture on Freud's dream theory as early as January 25th, 1900; the first Swiss psychoanalytic society, the Society of Freudian Studies, was founded in Zürich in 1907. The second branch originated in Geneva, where also in 1900 Theodore Flournoy reported on Freud's Interpretation of Dreams at the University of Geneva. Alexander Moser (1992) has traced the multitwisted history of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society from its beginnings up to the present, so I won't go into all the details here.

More important even than the language differences, cultural diversities constitute a major challenge to our society.

[[RunningHead]]

Developmentally Based Psychotherapy

Stanley Greenspan. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1997, 462 pp., $70.00.

Linda Mayes

Linda Mayes

230 S. Frontage Road
New Haven, CT 06520
Fax: 203-785-7926
E-mail: Linda.mayes@yale.edu

Linda Mayes   Arnold Gesell Associate Professor of Child Psychiatry, Pediatrics, and Psychology in the Yale Child Study Center; Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Like many of the developmental phenomena that it considers, Greenspan's Developmentally Based Psychotherapy is a multilayered experience and expression. At its most ambitious, the book offers an attempt to integrate the concept of basic developmental lines (such as affect regulation) into a theory of therapeutic action and then into a handbook of technique applicable to both children and adults. Similarly, equally ambitiously, the book implicitly proposes a longitudinal, predictive, and somewhat linear model linking the various functional difficulties of our adult patients to patterns of behaving, interacting, and experiencing set early in development. While both goals are perhaps sufficiently grand as to border on unrealistic and impractical, Greenspan's integration nonetheless offers some very important technical and theoretical insights for clinicians working with children or adults.

Perhaps the most basic of these insights is that a thorough understanding of the developmental precursors of phenomena such as the ability to represent emotions in a verbally nuanced manner is crucial to a therapist's capacity to tailor his or her therapeutic techniques to an individual's needs. Greenspan is not espousing a particular theoretical school, but rather insists that the idea of truly “developmentally” based therapeutic intervention applies to any number of theoretical perspectives from psychodynamic to cognitive-behavioral. In other words, “developmentally based psychotherapy” is an overarching theoretical perspective uniting or cutting across many schools of therapeutic technique and action. It is based on the clinician's ability to determine the level of a patient's ego or personality development and the presence of any deficits, delays, or deviations in those constructions. Interventions are guided by an appreciation of where and how the individual functions in this clinical developmental appraisal. In this sense, Greenspan offers a very practical approach to working with a whole spectrum of patients with functional difficulties, including those with so-called borderline organization. The practical utility of the book comes through most vividly in the clinical discussions.

The Social Work Psychoanalyst's Casebook: Clinical Voices in Honor of Jean Sanville

Edited by Joyce Edward and Elaine Rose. The Analytic Press: Hillsdale, NJ, 1999, 256 pp., $42.50.

Gail Glenn

Gail Glenn

225 South Meramec
Suite 208
Saint Louis, MO 63105

Gail Glenn   faculty, St. Louis Psychoanalytic Institute.

In The Social Work Psychoanalyst's Casebook editors Joyce Edward and Elaine Rose pay tribute to Jean Sanville as they reveal the rich interplay between social work and psychoanalysis. Nine clinical vignettes representing diverse orientations demonstrate Sanville's pioneering integration of psychoanalysis and psychosocial principles. This anthology illustrates the analyst's work with patients using time-honored social work precepts (e.g., “Begin where the client is and take a flexible, responsive, nonauthoritarian stance while respecting the uniqueness of the individual” (p. 203).

An interview with Sanville opens the book, affording a glimpse of the evolution of her analytic thought and of her professional identity as social work analyst, teacher, and author. Her influence is shown through the contributors as they strip away dogma and politics to pay close attention to the here and now, as well as to the intermediate region co-created by patient and therapist. The safe climate created by the therapist-patient dyad allows the patient the freedom to play with words, dreams, and images that can lead the therapist to interpretations. The patient's self-righting aspects and the role of positive emotions in promoting growth and development are presented as cornerstones of successful treatment.

Diversity in clinical practice and the integral relationship of theory and therapeutic activity are manifest throughout the case studies, which illustrate timely issues in analytic practice and reflect Sanville's emphasis on responding creatively to individuals and their situations.

333

Mankind's Oedipal Destiny: Libidinal and Aggressive Aspects of Sexuality

Edited by Peter Hartocollis. International Universities Press, 2001, ix +287 pp., $29.90 paperback.

Leon Anisfeld

Leon Anisfeld

750 Kappock Street #A2
Riverdale, NY 10463

Leon Anisfeld   voluntary Faculty and Clinical Assistant Professor, Departments of Psychiatry and Community Medicine, Mount Sinai School of Medicine, New York.

Mankind's Oedipal Destiny is a compilation of thirteen essays by well-known analysts on the subject of the oedipus complex, considered from various theoretical vantage points. The essays derive from papers originally presented as part of the International Psychoanalytic Symposium at Delphi, whose focus was the oedipus complex in modem psychoanalytic theory and practice.

1445

The essays range from theoretical and clinical to applied psychoanalytic in their focus, and they capture much of contemporary debate about the relevance of the oedipal complex to current formulations of theory and treatment. For example, in “Sexuality in Nonneurotic Structures: Yesterday and Today,” André Green points out that while sexuality remains central to analytic conceptions of neurosis, when we discuss preoedipal conditions we tend to switch theoretical frameworks, abandoning Freudian drive theory in favor of theories that focus on ego functioning and object relations.

In his discussion of the Wolf Man, Freud implicitly connected regression to anal eroticism with a level of mental functioning that relies on splitting and disavowal, “primitive defenses” that typify preoedipal states and also the sexual perversions. Green proposes that in nonneurotic conditions genitality and genitalization mask pregenital fixations and, conversely, that oral and anal preoccupations can conceal genital fixations in the more disturbed patient.

Hanna Segal's essay discusses the place of the oedipal complex in Kleinian theory, emphasizing its early developmental appearance. The importance and acceptance of Kleinian thinking in our conceptualizations about the more disturbed patient are echoed in a number of other essays in this volume.

In his well-known essay integrating Freudian and Kleinian perspectives, Otto Kernberg discusses some aspects of object relations theory as related to the drives. This focus is taken up again in his work on the role of affects in the integration and development of self and object relationships. Kernberg's essay in the volume under review, “The Concept of Libido in the Light of Contemporary Psychoanalytic Theorizing,” is important in understanding his later work.

Treating Addiction as a Human Process

Edward J. Khantzian. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1999, 687 pp., $70.00.

Brian Johnson

Brian Johnson

Five Park Place
Newton, MA 02460
E-mail: Bjohnson@massmed.org

Brian Johnson   Assistant Clinical Professor, Harvard Medical School; Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Treating Addiction as a Human Process contains the collected works of Edward Khantzian, thirty-seven articles or book chapters published between 1972 and 1997. Khantzian has added three new chapters, made minor modifications to previously published works, and provided introductions to each of the five sections. His target audience includes patients and their families, as well as students and clinicians. His goal of inclusiveness, both in terms of material and readership, makes this a valuable sourcebook.

During three decades of work with underserved and stigmatized patients, Khantzian has maintained that his psychoanalytic sensibility is his strongest asset; as suggested in the book's title, addiction is a human process. “Drug dependence,” he writes, “is intimately tied to the individual's attempt to cope with his or her internal emotional and external social and physical environment” (p. 65). Accordingly, he uses the empathic treatment relationship as “a valuable source of information for identifying and understanding the psychological vulnerabilities of addicts and how such vulnerabilities might motivate a reliance on drugs” (p. 204).

Khantzian, a master of the addiction literature, is able to cite the nonanalytic literature in support of his work. At one point he states, “In this section I sample material from the genetic-behavioral and psychodiagnostic perspective and compare them to a clinical perspective with a psychoanalytic orientation”; he asserts that his contributions are “complementary rather than competitive” (p. 369).

With relatively few analytic colleagues, Khantzian has been able to make psychoanalysis a force in the field of addiction. Several of his most enduring contributions deserve specific mention. The self-medication hypothesis, which appeared in its final form as a lead article in the Am. J. Psychiatry in 1985

[[RunningHead]]

Shame/Contempt Interchanges

A Frequent Component of the Analyst-Patient Interaction

Gail Reed

Gail Reed

Suite I-G
1199 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: Reed1172@compuserve.com
Fax: 212-722-4077

Gail Reed  

The task of this panel was to go beyond the topic of shame to address its much less scrutinized interrelation with contempt. Although the subject conjures up psychodynamic formulations concerning the component instincts of sadism and masochism, no such paired opposites, or the unconscious fantasies linked to them, were invoked by the title of the panel. Instead, the words interchange and interaction emphasized the reality of interpersonal activity and left to the panelists any psychodynamic explanations for what was observed. The very pairing of these two affects, however, posed questions concerning the place of aggression in human development, the dynamics and mechanisms involved with role reversal in the history of an individual's object relations, the relation of observed affects to functions of defense and to unconscious fantasy, and the place of projective identification.

Joseph Lichtenberg, the first panelist to present material, described observable interchanges, emphasizing the subtlety not only of the patient's possible expression of shame, but of the responsive presence of contempt, frequently unacknowledged, in the analyst. “In any dyadic relationship,” he contended, “one person's persistent shame is apt to trigger contempt in the other.” He suggested that shame and contempt can emerge in the patient as responses to the analyst's subjective limitations and that both feelings, in either member of the dyad, tend to be enacted if they go unrecognized by the analyst.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Washington, DC, May 15, 1999.

Panelists: Benjamin Kilborne (chair), Joseph Lichtenberg, Barnet Malin, Andrew Morrison, Leon Wurmser. Discussant: Gail S. Reed.

responseSet.title: Controversial Discussions
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Controversial Discussions

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

5225 Connecticut Avenue
Washington, DC 20015-1845
E-mail: wspmd@erols.com

Warren Poland  

Open disciplined debate is the best device scholarship has for protecting itself against descent into scholasticism. Yet psychoanalysts, like all who inhabit the domain of ideas, are ever vulnerable to letting a desire for personal primacy overwhelm the deeper goal of expanding the realm of knowledge.

Although often thought of as a static body of accumulated information, science is a living being. It absorbs new knowledge, struggles to digest and assimilate that knowledge, changing its shape and growing, at times at a glacial pace, at others in dizzying leaps. Whatever the wonders that contribute to its growth, science is a product of the human mind, a body of knowledge that exists, and matters, only as it resides in and is dealt with by the human mind.

Even though new ideas may defy earlier understandings, they are born in the context of past knowledge. As a result, new views are not always welcomed with open arms. The more threatening the new understandings, the more likely they will face the wall of a “compact majority.” Psychoanalysis has known this experience since its inception, a fact that likely accounts for much of the persistence of the public claim that psychoanalysis is dead.

Even within the professional arena, some discussions are singled out as especially controversial; those between the followers of Anna Freud and Melanie Klein are doubtless the best known instance of this. And how can we account for the intensity of such debates? Is it to be dismissed as mere growing pains, a difficulty in accommodating to spurts of growth once a body moves beyond childhood into burgeoning adolescence?

Perhaps, but surely there is more that accounts for the intensity of emotion in our conceptual battles. Workers care, and care deeply. When embattled, we tend too quickly—in our own vanity—to impute narcissism to others as the motive of their arguments. Quite naturally, we care most about ideas we have ourselves conceived, labored to deliver, struggled to refine. However, more than narcissism is in play.

Dianne Elise   Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Associate Editor, Studies for Gender and Sexuality.

To compile this anthology, editor Donna Bassin invited fifteen recognized writers on gender to select a classic psychoanalytic paper that has been a personal favorite. These writers each introduce their choice by describing to the reader the influence of the paper on their own thinking about female development. These commentaries are engaging and fresh and often have an almost conversational tone that gives the reader a palpable sense of listening to a respected colleague speaking about the impact of a key paper on his or her thinking. The special quality of this approach draws the reader into a relationship with both the commentators and the authors of the original papers.

The Musical Edge of Therapeutic Dialogue

Steven H. Knoblauch. Hillside, NJ: Analytic Press, 2000, 184 pp. $33.50.

Ephi Betan

Ephi Betan

990 Hammond Drive, 11th Floor
Atlanta, GA 30328
Fax: 770-671-0476
Email: Ebetan@gspp.edu

Ephi Betan   Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute; Assistant Professor and Director of Training, Georgia School of Professional Psychology.

In psychoanalytic writing, a good metaphor can be a basis for elaborating clinical techniqu and shaping alternative modes of relating in the therapeutic dyad. In The Musical Edge of Therapeutic Dialogue, Steven Knoblauch offers music as a metaphor for conceptualizing the nonverbal dimensions of communication as rhythms of relational exchange that carry the resonance of unformulated emotional experiences. In speaking of the melodic dimension of therapeutic engagement, he directs our attention to the how of speech—tone, rhythm, and cadence—and how these reverberate in body and emotion within and between patient and analyst.

Just as music renders affect by both inducing and framing emotions, so do the nonverbal nuances of interaction—breathing, body movement, tone and rhythm of speech. Knoblauch shows how an analyst attending to rhythm, volume, and tone can recognize important shifts in affect and thereby “hear” in the process the powerful unconscious representations of self and other, as well as the “dissociated affect states” that shape the interchange. The musical edge of therapeutic dialogue is a metaphor for the connections and disconnections of relatedness, as the exchange can be one of harmony and accompaniment, or one of being out of rhythm with each other. Empathy and emotional regulation in the analytic space emerge in concert as patient and analyst find one another's rhythms, harmonize, and improvise.

1064

Immigration and Identity: Turmoil, Treatment, and Transformation

Salman Akhtar. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1999, 240 pp., $40.00.

César Garza-Guerrero

César Garza-Guerrero

Orinoco 335 Ote. Col. del Valle
Sn. Pedro, Garza García, N.L.
México 66220
Fax: +52-8-356-4971
E-mail: Cegagro@infosel.net.mx

César Garza-Guerrero   Training and Supervising Analyst, Monterrey Psychoanalytic Society; Chairman, Department of Psychiatry, University Hospital, Monterrey, NL, México.

Although immigration has always been an essential aspect of the human condition, the globalization and technical advances of the modern world have brought it to the center of our attention. However, this is still a largely unexplored field in psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. Salman Akhtar's lucid and perceptive book, therefore, constitutes an opportune and valuable contribution to the study of one of the

1081

most pressing everyday challenges of our present world. Akhtar's empathy for his subject (derived partly from his own experience as an immigrant), his clarity of understanding, and his poetic nature make of his book a splendid contribution to a systematized and integrated comprehension of the mourning processes and vicissitudes of identity that are demanded by drastic changes in sociocultural and psychological environment.

Immigration is a complex psychosocial phenomenon; it mobilizes a multifaceted process of destabilization/restabilization with outcomes that may range along a continuum from the normal to the highly pathological. In chapter 1, Akhtar elucidates ten psychosocial variables that may influence these outcomes. Among these are: circumstances and reasons for migration; access to “refueling”; preimmigration character; intercultural marriages; and birth of children in the new land. In addition, Akhtar explores seven other variables largely unaddressed in the immigration literature, such as: the role of bodily characteristics; the impact of gender (e.g., women seem to adapt much better to immigration); and legal status. Among all these variables Akhtar rightly underscores the significance of character organization (as opposed to character traits): i.e., the capacity for intrapsychic separateness and individuation. I myself would think it more precise to refer to this variable as the level of personality organization: that is, the degree of differentiation and integration of personality development (Kernberg 1984; McWilliams 1994; Garza-Guerrero 2000).

In chapter 2, Akhtar reviews the historical evolution of the concept of identity. He also discusses its developmental origins (from what seems to be a psychostructural view of object relationships), its phenomenological manifestations, and its clinical relevance for psychiatry, psychoanalysis, and clinical psychology. This review serves in turn as an introduction to chapter 3, which I think represents a major contribution of this book, and which analyzes through the delineation of four interlinked developmental dimensions the transformation of identity upon immigration. These dimensions are: drive and affect (from love and hate to ambivalence); interpersonal and psychic space (from near or far to optimal distance); temporality (from yesterday or tomorrow to today); and social affiliation (from yours or mine to ours).

The Sadomasochism of Everyday Life: Why We Hurt Ourselves—and Others—and How to Stop

John Munder Ross. New York: Simon and Schuster, 1997, 229 pp., $17.95.

Kerry Novick

Kerry Novick

617 Stratford Drive
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
Fax: 734-665-2875
E-mail: Jnovick@umich.edu

Kerry Novick   Child and Adolescent Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; faculty, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Director, Allen Creek Preschool.

John Munder Ross's The Sadomasochism of Everyday Life pushes the reader to consider several important issues. It is admirable in its ambition to grapple with and explain clinical and social phenomena that have long confounded psychoanalytic theory and practice. Ross tries to elucidate sadomasochism in ordinary life; its clinical manifestations, including perversions; its origins and functions in the individual and in society; and ways to address it both therapeutically and societally.

The argument of this book is that sadomasochism is endemic to the human condition, universal in development, and ubiquitous in personal and social relationships. The task of development and life is to conquer the temptations of sadomasochism and to resist the pull of regression to what Ross conceptualizes as the inevitable sadomasochistic substratum of the personality: “Because of the nature of infantile sexuality, then, sexual masochism is an inevitable byproduct of desire and frustration” (p. 140).

1459

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Art, Freud and Leonardo

Harold Blum

Harold Blum

23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn Estates, NY 11576
Fax: 516-621-3014
E-mail: haroldpblum@cs.com

Harold Blum  

Freud was the first to apply psychoanalysis to art, choosing for his subject the life and work of Leonardo da Vinci. Observing Leonardo's partly fused image of the Virgin and St. Anne, he inferred that the artist had depicted his two mothers, his biological mother and his stepmother. This very early analytic discourse on parent loss and adoption changed the course of the interpretation of art. Freud explored the psychology of art, the artist, and aesthetic appreciation. Confronting the age-old enigma of the Mona Lisa, he proposed a daring solution to the riddle of the sphinxlike smile of this icon of art. His paper prefigures concepts of narcissism, homosexuality, parenting, and sublimation. Lacking modern methodology and theory, Freud's pioneering insights overshadow his naive errors. In this fledgling inquiry, based on a childhood screen memory and limited knowledge of Leonardo's artistic and scientific contributions, Freud identified with this Renaissance genius in his own self-analytic and creative endeavor.

The Disregarded Analyst and the Transgressive Process

Discontinuity, Countertransference, and the Framing of the Negative

Gail Reed

Gail Reed

1199 Park Avenue
Suite 1G
New York, NY 10128-1711
E-mail: Gail.reed@aya.yale.edu

Gail Reed  

This paper describes the difficulty of working with patients who have adopted conflict solutions common to perversion. These analysands' rapid shifts from one discontinuous mental state to another draw the analyst into a regressive transference/countertransference engagement characterized by alternations of actualized self and object representations. The analyst's disengagement from this regressive interaction is crucial, but difficult. A detailed clinical example illustrates the process of disengagement as well as the transformation of this transference into a more traditionally consolidated one. A technical approach is suggested and questions of representation and symbolization are explored.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Jeffery

Why Psychoanalysts Have Low Mortality Rates

Norman Doidge

Norman Doidge

180 Bloor Street West, Suite 501
Toronto, Ontario
M5S 2V6, Canada
Fax: 416-783-8556
E-mail: Norman.doidge@utoronto.ca

Norman Doidge  

Hope deferred maketh a sick heart

But desire fulfilled is as the Tree of Life.

—Proverbs 13:12

Little things say a lot: psychoanalysts turn out to have extremely low mortality rates, lower than almost any other occupation.

This finding is reported in the following paper by Edward Jeffery. The study compares the mortality rates of male psychoanalysts to that of white American males generally, and to those of other occupations for the thirty-year period 1953-1982, a period for which data were available. Jeffery found that psychoanalysts have a mortality rate 48 percent lower than that of the male population at large, which means that in a given year they have half the chance of dying as has the average American male.

The magnitude of this difference is impressive. It is measured using the standardized mortality ratio (SMR), in which an actual death rate of a group forms the numerator, and the death rate of a comparable population the denominator (the higher the SMR, the higher the death rate). According to Jeffery, the SMR for male analysts was within the lowest 4 percent among the 124 white male occupational groups listed in “Mortality by Occupation and Industry,” a U.S. government publication.

This finding is not attributable simply to the fact that analysts have higher-than-average educational and income levels, two factors that correlate with lower mortality. The psychoanalysts in the study were also physicians, yet had lower mortality rates than physicians with comparable years of education, and similar or even higher incomes.

The analysts in the study had all qualified as physicians, as psychiatrists, and as psychoanalysts accredited in the American Psychoanalytic Association.

Titration in the Treatment of the More Troubled Patient

Jerome Winer and Eric Ornstein

Eric Ornstein

912 S. Wood Street
Chicago, IL 60612
Fax: 312-355-0757
E-mail: Jwiner@psych.uic.edu

Eric Ornstein  

This article defines and discusses a modification of technique recommended by the authors in the psychoanalytic treatment of more troubled patients—a modification they call titration. Titration is defined as a conscious decision by the analyst to increase or decrease assistance (or gratification) gradually, in order to facilitate the analytic process. The authors emphasize the complexity of decisions in treatment by focusing on the decision-making processes that titration requires. Guidelines and a case vignette are presented. The authors conclude by considering some of the politics involved in the introduction of technique modifications, the salience of the titration concept, and directions for further exploration.

responseSet.title: Knowledge and Authority: The Godfather Fantasy
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Knowledge and Authority

The Godfather Fantasy

Ethel Person

Ethel Person

135 Central Park West
New York, NY 10023
Espersonny@aol.com

Ethel Person  

Three strategies are tentatively proposed by which human beings try to assuage the need for transcendence: (1) seizing the godhead; (2) submission to God; and (3) the godfather fantasy, whereby an attempt is made to clothe a mere mortal in the cloak of godly power. These strategies are connected to our innate, often conflicted, tendencies to obedience and rebellion, later elaborated into submission to authority or resistance to or cooptation of authority. These psychic tendencies are illustrated in Dostoevsky's “The Legend of the Grand Inquisitor” and in Mario Puzo's The Godfather, both brilliant demonstrations of social hierarchical control. The almost universal pull to obedience is demonstrated in the research of Stanley Milgram on obedience in hierarchical situations and theorized by Freud in his formulation of “the thirst for obedience” as a product of infantile life. It is proposed that the function of the group mind, in the form of ideology, is to counter anxieties generated throughout life, not only the remnants of childhood anxiety but also fears of death and oblivion. That a person's will to power can get out of hand and lead to disaster hardly needs saying; yet the will to submission, when connected to a shared ideology, can lead to results no less devastating. More people are killed in the name of an ideological cause run amok than are destroyed by virtue of thwarted passion, personal vendettas, criminality, or greed.

Thomas Müller. Frankfurt am Main: Deja Vu, 2000, 344 pp.

Peter Neubauer

Peter Neubauer

33 East 70th Street
New York NY 10021
Fax: 212-472-3039

Peter Neubauer   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, NYU College of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute.

The title of this book—in English, From Charlottenburg to Central Park West: Henry Lowenfeld and Psychoanalysis in Berlin, Prague, and New York—could be misleading if the reader expects a standard biography of the analyst Henry Lowenfeld. It is the belief of both the author, Thomas Müller, and of his subject that the life history of any

329

person is the product of the environmental conditions that favor some and inhibit other features of personality development.

This position requires, in this instance, a careful study of the Zeitgeist, the conditions of life in Berlin, especially at the Berlin Psychoanalytic Institute, until 1933; of the Prague Psychoanalytic Study Group up to 1938; and, finally, of the period of Lowenfeld's membership in the New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Thus, this book gives us insight into the historical conditions of the life of a German Jew who, with so many others, became an analyst and was influenced by the political theories of his time. He was influenced particularly by Marxism, as formulated by Herbert Marcuse, and by Otto Fenichel, who with Edith Jacobson, Annie and Wilhelm Reich, Erich Fromm, and George Gero created a Marxian psychoanalytic seminar in Central Europe in the early 1930s.

Given this background, it is not surprising that Henry Lowenfeld established a friendly relationship with Alexander Mitscherlich, or that he was critical of the Americanization of psychoanalysis. He had in mind, however, not the development of Hartmann's ego psychology but rather the stress on clinical pathology—oriented psychotherapy to the neglect of exploring the effect of social conditions on psychic life.

Müller's intention is to present a sociohistorically oriented biography; he does not claim to have written a psychobiography. He is interested in the history of psychoanalysis, primarily in order to understand its origin, development, and application. In his introduction, he refers to his wish to understand the difference between the German and the American approach to expanding psychoanalysis; to explore new theories emerging in the application and interpretation of Freud's work; and to examine what happened to psychoanalysts who held “political positions.” It is his belief that political orientation and the formation of psychoanalytic theories are both dependent on the Lebenswelt, or life-world, of those involved.

Margaret Crastnopol   Associate Director, Northwest Center for Psychoanalysis, Seattle; Supervisor of Psychotherapy and faculty, William Alanson White Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, New York; Clinical Instructor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences and Department of Psychology, University of Washington.

Relationality is the most recent addition to the late Stephen Mitchell's twenty-year project of articulating a theoretical position in which the intrapsychic and the interpersonal mutually shape and enrich one another, as if they were an “endless Mobius strip” (p. 53) of the self. The book's preface (which actually has the more substantial character and function of an introduction) sets forth the goal of exploring the interface among the differing relational perspectives currently being advanced. Mitchell sees these views—each with its own provenance—as representing different dimensions of relational experience, rather than as alternative hypotheses about what relationality truly is. His overarching aim is to uncover the underlying commonalities while acknowledging the differences in these views, to set them in meaningful counterpoint with each other, and ultimately to organize them in a way that makes it easier to choose among them in the heat of the clinical moment. A related purpose is to strengthen the author's epistemological argument that relationality is ingrained in human existence “by design,” and that relational attachments are therefore more fundamental than drives in patterning internal psychic life. I found the book highly successful in achieving these goals.

The book's six essays are divided into two sections, “From Ghosts to Ancestors: The Psychoanalytic Vision of Hans Loewald” and “Levels of Organization.

Steven Cooper begins his new project of comparing analytic persuasions—the American relational and ego-psychological, the British object relations and the contemporary Kleinian—by making an obvious but often overlooked observation: that “psychoanalysis has a passion for the possible.” This statement captures well Cooper's approach to the problems created by analysts' reluctance to acknowledge their hopes and wishes for their patients in clinical work, and the limitations of psychoanalysis in helping their patients transform their lives.

Cooper's most cogent exploration of the role of hope and limitation in psychoanalysis comes in the final two chapters of his book. In the last chapter, he discusses how theory functions as a logic of hope, providing a containing function for analysts as they cope with the frustrations of the unexplainable and the intractable aspects of their patients.

This issue of the leading German psychoanalytic journal Psyche deals in a very impressive and thorough philosophical way with current controversies around the topics of subject, identity, and history; each of the studies published here counters some prominent current trends toward negating the psychoanalytic relevance of these three. (All passages quoted in this review have been translated by me.)

Werner Bohleber (who contributed the paper “Psychoanalysis, Adolescence, and the Problem of Identity”), the editor of Psyche, refutes a fashionable trend (represented by, among others, Bollas, Hinshelwood, Schafer, and Laplanche) that wants to move psychoanalysis to a “postmodern criticism of the subject” by advocating the “dissolution of identity and the death of the subject” or declaring “the category of multiple personality disorder as a criterion of the current epoch” (p. 517). “The decentering and deconstructive potency of the analytic method represents to them [Strenger and Laplanche] a congenial approach for understanding the modern self; it recognizes its unity as an illusion and understands itself as decentered, as being constantly in flux and open for revisions” (p. 509). For Schafer, “the self is a narrative and requires a listener in order to exist.” For Hinshelwood it is “an interpersonal negotiation about what is being ascribed to self and others by splitting and projective identification” (p. 512). For Bollas, “the idea of a unitary self is an illusion … a kind of false consciousness expressing the wish of the individual for unification and integration.” (Similar ideas could, by the way, be adduced from Nietzsche and Dostoevsky).

Bohleber rightly counters that in this perspective the function of affects vanishes: “They cannot just be reduced to narrative experience but are founded in body processes. Moreover, the role of traumatic

Freud described female sexuality as receptive, claiming normal women could be only passive objects, not active subjects. With a nod to Luis Bunuel's That Obscure Object of Desire, Ronnie C. Lesser and Erica Schoenberg's collection of postmodernist essays argues against that position. The essays are a commentary on Freud's “The Psychogenesis of a Case of Homosexuality in a Woman” (1920) “from both psychoanalysts and academic scholars”; according to Lester, “This is a much-needed conversation; psychoanalysts have long been isolated from scholars in other fields, and academicians are often not acquainted with the work of contemporary psychoanalysts” (p. 1).
